title: Official Poverty Means Mass Starvation
socialistClaimText: With 11% of Americans in poverty, capitalism has failed millions — we need socialism to guarantee basic needs.
executiveSummary: The Census official poverty measure (OPM) is a relative income threshold, not a nutrition or shelter deprivation census. It ignores non-cash benefits (SNAP, housing vouchers, Medicaid), tax credits (EITC), and household composition. Supplemental Poverty Measure (SPM) — also published by Census — counts resources more completely and consistently shows lower poverty than OPM when transfers are included. Equating OPM headcount with Victorian immiseration misreads what the statistic measures.
evidenceBullets: U.S. Census Bureau (2024): official poverty rate 11.1% in 2023 — a narrow money-income threshold, not a measure of hunger or homelessness.
Census SPM reports: supplemental measure incorporates taxes and in-kind transfers; SPM poverty often differs materially from OPM.
USDA Food Security Survey (2023): most low-income households are food secure; food insecurity rates are far below OPM poverty rates.
CBO (2022): means-tested transfers and refundable credits raise bottom-quintile resources substantially — omitted from OPM headlines.

whyItMatters: Campaigns cite OPM as proof of systemic collapse while ignoring transfers that already flow to low-income households. Honest poverty debate requires specifying the measure and what it excludes.

title: The Top 1% Own Everything
socialistClaimText: The richest 1% hoard nearly all American wealth while everyone else owns nothing — proof the system is rigged.
executiveSummary: Federal Reserve Survey of Consumer Finances (SCF) is the authoritative U.S. wealth microdata. Top-1% wealth share is elevated but the bottom 50% still hold trillions in net worth, largely housing and pension wealth. Wealth is stock (assets minus debts), not annual income — conflating the two produces outrage headlines that SCF tables do not support. Median net worth has risen over decades even as top shares grew.
evidenceBullets: Federal Reserve SCF (2023): distributional financial accounts and triennial survey document wealth shares by percentile — top 1% elevated but not 'everything.'
Fed Bulletin — Changes in U.S. Family Finances: median net worth and homeownership remain widespread middle-class balance-sheet anchors.
CBO wealth analysis: wealth concentration partly reflects age/lifecycle accumulation — older cohorts hold more assets by design.
BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey: durable goods ownership (vehicles, appliances) widespread outside top decile.

chartData.title: Share of U.S. Net Worth by Wealth Percentile (Fed SCF, illustrative)

title: Real Wages Have Stagnated for Decades
socialistClaimText: Workers haven't gotten a real raise since the 1970s — all gains went to bosses and shareholders.
executiveSummary: BLS Employment Cost Index and average hourly earnings series show real compensation growth over multi-decade windows when deflated appropriately and including benefits. Stagnation narratives often cherry-pick a single deflator, exclude benefits, or use median vs. mean inconsistently. BEA personal income data corroborate rising real consumption possibilities for households over time.
evidenceBullets: BLS Employment Cost Index: total compensation (wages + benefits) for civilian workers has risen in real terms over long horizons.
BLS Average Hourly Earnings deflated by CPI: episodic stagnation around recessions, not a flat 50-year line.
BEA Personal Income and Outlays: real disposable personal income per capita trends upward over decades.
CBO (2019 distribution): average real market income per household rose 1979–2019 across quintiles — inconsistent with universal wage freeze.

title: America Does Not Redistribute
socialistClaimText: Unlike Europe, America lets the poor starve while the rich keep every dollar — we need Nordic-style redistribution.
executiveSummary: CBO's distributional accounts explicitly model federal taxes and transfers. Bottom-quintile households receive a large net subsidy as a share of market income; top quintiles pay net positive taxes. U.S. redistribution is less aggressive than Denmark's, but claiming zero redistribution falsifies CBO tables. Debate should compare marginal rates, transfer design, and growth outcomes — not a fiction of no safety net.
evidenceBullets: CBO (2022): average income after transfers and taxes rose for all quintiles 1979–2019; bottom quintile +94% real.
CBO supplemental tables: lowest quintile market income vs. after-transfer income shows large fiscal footprint.
OECD Social Expenditure Database: U.S. public social spending is lower than Nordics but material — not absent.
Census SPM: incorporates EITC, SNAP, housing assistance — poverty lower than OPM when counted.

title: The Middle Class Cannot Build Wealth
socialistClaimText: Homeownership and retirement accounts are myths for ordinary Americans — only the elite accumulate assets.
executiveSummary: Fed SCF and Census American Housing Survey document broad homeownership and pension participation. Primary residence remains the largest asset for middle quintiles; 401(k)/IRA balances have grown with defined-contribution shift. Affordability crises in select metros are real policy failures — they do not negate nationwide balance-sheet data showing widespread asset building.
evidenceBullets: Census AHS (2023): majority of U.S. households own their home; home equity is the dominant middle-class asset.
Fed SCF: retirement account ownership spans income distribution; median balances rise with age cohort.
Federal Reserve Bulletin: net worth growth 2019–2022 included broad-based gains, not only top decile.
BLS National Compensation Survey: employer-sponsored retirement coverage remains common in medium/large firms.

title: High Housing Costs Prove Capitalism Failed
socialistClaimText: Rent and home prices crush the working class because landlords and developers exploit housing — socialism would guarantee affordable homes.
executiveSummary: Economists across the spectrum attribute high prices in productive metros primarily to supply restrictions: zoning, permitting delays, impact fees, and growth controls. Glaeser & Gyourko document how land-use regulation raises prices; Chetty's mobility work links restrictive zoning to lower upward mobility. Venezuela-style rent control and nationalization produced shortages — market supply expansion, not abolishing property rights, is the evidence-based fix.
evidenceBullets: Glaeser & Gyourko (NBER): stringent land-use regulation correlates with higher house prices and lower construction.
Chetty & Hendren mobility literature: areas with more restrictive zoning show worse outcomes for low-income children.
Census Building Permits Survey: permitting volumes vary sharply by jurisdiction — political supply constraints, not inherent market malice.
World Bank cross-country data: nations with secure property rights and functioning mortgage markets expand housing stock faster over time.

title: Only the Rich Start Businesses
socialistClaimText: Entrepreneurship is a fantasy for workers — you need inherited wealth to start a business in America.
executiveSummary: Census Bureau Business Dynamics Statistics and SBA Office of Advocacy data show millions of new firm births annually, with small businesses employing nearly half the private workforce. Kauffman Foundation research documents that many founders start with modest capital and personal savings, not dynastic wealth. Chetty's inventor and entrepreneur mobility studies link innovation rates to exposure and education — policy levers, not abolishing profit, expand access.
evidenceBullets: U.S. Census BDS: hundreds of thousands of employer firm births per year; job creation disproportionately from young firms.
SBA Advocacy (2024): 33.2 million small businesses; 61.7 million employees — not an elite-only sector.
Kauffman Index of Startup Activity: entry rates fluctuate but remain material across cycles.
Chetty et al. — 'Who Becomes an Inventor?': innovation rates rise sharply with childhood exposure to inventors — an opportunity problem, not fixed caste.

title: r > g Means Inevitable Oligarchy
socialistClaimText: Piketty proved that returns on capital always outpace growth, so wealth must concentrate forever unless we seize capital.
executiveSummary: Piketty's r > g inequality (Capital in the Twenty-First Century) is a conditional historical correlation, not a law of nature. Critics including the OECD, IMF working papers, and McCloskey note sensitivity to periods, housing treatment, and missing human capital. U.S. Fed SCF and World Bank data show wealth shares move with policy, inflation, and asset bubbles — not a deterministic march to oligarchy. Confiscation risks the growth that lifts absolute living standards.
evidenceBullets: Piketty (2014) r > g: empirical claim debated; pre-WWI and post-1980 samples behave differently.
OECD (2015) analysis: wealth inequality drivers include housing, inheritances, and financial assets — policy-responsive.
IMF staff discussion: relationship between r, g, and inequality is heterogeneous across countries.
World Bank / Our World in Data: global poverty fell during decades when capital returns were positive — growth and returns coexist.
fallacies: confusing model with law of nature

title: Inequality Means Injustice
socialistClaimText: Any economic inequality is theft — fairness requires equal outcomes.
claimQuote: When plunder becomes a way of life for a group of men in a society, over the course of time they create for themselves a legal system that authorizes it and a moral code that glorifies it.
quoteAttribution: Frédéric Bastiat, The Law (1850) — public domain
executiveSummary: Equating all dispersion with theft collapses voluntary exchange, risk compensation, and lifecycle savings into 'plunder.' Bastiat distinguished just property from legalized expropriation — the relevant injustice is coercion, not a Gini score. Census and World Bank absolute metrics show rising living standards across quintiles while shares differ. Egalitarian outcomes enforced by state power historically produced the famines documented in the Historical Socialism category.
evidenceBullets: Bastiat, The Law (1850, PD): justice is defense of person and property; 'legal plunder' is state-coerced transfer — not voluntary market dispersion.
CBO (2022): real after-tax incomes rose for all quintiles — dispersion coexisted with broad gains, inconsistent with pure theft framing.
World Bank extreme poverty series: absolute deprivation fell while within-country inequality varied — outcomes and dispersion are distinct moral questions.
Heritage Index of Economic Freedom: societies with secure property rights score higher on prosperity composites than equal-outcome dictatorships.

sources.title: Bastiat — The Law (1850)
sources.citation: Bastiat, F. (1850). The Law. Liberty Fund Online Library of Liberty.

whyItMatters: Moralizing all inequality as theft justifies unlimited coercion. Bastiat's frame — distinguish voluntary exchange from legal plunder — keeps debate honest.
